text.title: Notion Views and Automations
text.summary: Board, table, timeline, and calendar view configurations; native automation patterns; Zapier/n8n integration.

rule.statement: Create four purpose-driven views per database: Board (sprint execution, group by Status), Table (all tasks, sort by priority), Timeline (roadmap, group by Project), Calendar (deadlines, filter: Status not Done).
rule.rationale: Each view serves a distinct PM function; proliferating views with overlapping purposes creates confusion about which view is authoritative.

example.note: Board view: filter Sprint = Current Sprint, group by Status, show Assignee/Priority/Estimate/Due Date properties on cards.

antipattern.description: Creating a new view for every sprint or team member.
antipattern.reason: Views multiply without a governance policy; use filters on shared views instead of creating per-person or per-sprint views.

rule.statement: Use native Notion automations only for property-triggered actions: set Completion Date when Status is Done, notify Assignee when Priority is Urgent, set Status to Backlog on page creation.
rule.rationale: Native automations are limited to property triggers — they cannot run on a schedule. For time-based jobs (daily standup digest, sprint closure), use n8n, Make, or a cron-based agent.

rule.statement: Never delete sprint pages — archive them by setting a Status property to "Completed". Deleted sprint pages break any task's Sprint relation field that pointed to them.
rule.rationale: Notion relation fields that point to deleted pages become null silently; rollup properties that count sprint tasks stop working. Archived pages preserve all existing relations.

rule.statement: Use n8n (self-hostable) or Make for time-triggered Notion workflows: daily standup digest (query tasks in sprint where Status = Blocked or In Progress → post to Slack), sprint closure (compute metrics → fill Sprint page).
rule.rationale: n8n is compatible with the NERO stack and can be self-hosted; it handles the Notion API rate limit (3 req/sec) with built-in retry logic.
